text.title: Chunking Strategy Types
text.summary: Eight chunking strategies for RAG ingestion, with selection criteria, parameters, and a decision framework.

rule.statement: Apply this decision framework: document < 1000 tokens → no chunking; is code → code-aware; is structured (MD/HTML) → structure-based; context-critical (legal/medical) → semantic or agentic; variable content → agentic; default → recursive with 15% overlap.
rule.rationale: Each strategy has a distinct cost/quality/speed profile. Using the wrong one (e.g., fixed-size on legal text) causes mid-sentence splits that break retrieval for entire clauses.

rule.statement: Fixed-size chunking: 512 tokens, 15% overlap. Use only for uniform plain-text where speed is critical or as a baseline.
rule.rationale: Fast and predictable but ignores semantic boundaries. A sentence split across two chunks means neither retrieves well for that concept.

rule.statement: Recursive chunking: split by paragraph → line → sentence → word (in order). This is the default for general documents. Use 512-token chunks with 77-token overlap (15%).
rule.rationale: Respects natural text boundaries without requiring an embedding API. The best default for articles, blog posts, and mixed documentation.

rule.statement: Semantic chunking: embed each sentence, split where cosine similarity drops below 0.75-0.85. Use for multi-topic documents, legal, medical, or technical content where topical coherence is critical.
rule.rationale: Preserves topic coherence within chunks, improving retrieval precision. Costs one embedding API call per sentence — only justified when quality matters more than ingestion cost.

rule.statement: Code-aware chunking: split at function/class boundaries using AST or treesitter. Never split code at fixed token counts.
rule.rationale: A function split across two chunks is nearly impossible to retrieve correctly. Code retrieval depends on the function being a complete unit.

rule.statement: Late chunking (Jina jina-embeddings-v3): embed the full document first, then pool per-span. Use when document fits in model context and pronoun/reference resolution matters.
rule.rationale: Chunk embeddings carry document-level context ("the city" knows it means Berlin). Outperforms all rule-based strategies for contextual coherence but requires a specific long-context model.

rule.statement: Agentic chunking: LLM decides strategy per document section and enriches chunks with metadata (titles, summaries). Use only when quality >> cost — one LLM call per document section.
rule.rationale: Highest quality, adaptive, adds rich metadata. Costs 10-50x more than rule-based approaches. Reserve for legal contracts, medical reports, financial filings.
